message.content: Adversaries may exploit the Windows authentication process by registering malicious password filter DLLs to capture user credentials. These filters, designed to enforce password policies, can be implemented on local computers or domain controllers. When a password is validated, the Local Security Authority (LSA) sends plain-text credentials to each registered filter. Malicious filters can thus intercept these credentials. This technique can compromise both local and domain accounts, posing significant security risks. Notable examples include Remsec and Strider, which use this method to harvest credentials from domain controllers. Mitigation strategies include monitoring and validating registered password filters, employing strong access controls, and using endpoint detection and response (EDR) tools to detect suspicious DLL registrations. Regular audits and integrity checks of critical system files and configurations can also help prevent unauthorized modifications.
